planting.note: Stratify seed, or divide in spring; likes sandy soil.

directions: This compact native thrives in dry, sandy, acidic soils and full sun. Leathery basal leaves anchor low clumps that spread slowly. Large violet-blue daisies with golden centers make a striking late-season show.

planting.note: Stratify seed; needs very well-drained, lean soil.

directions: A gem for dry prairies and rock gardens, its silvery, silky-haired leaves shimmer all season. Give it lean, gravelly soil and full sun; it will rot in rich or wet ground. Deep violet daisies with yellow eyes stud the plant in autumn.

planting.note: Stratify seed; vigorous and best in moist meadows.

directions: This adaptable native handles moist to average soils and spreads by rhizomes, so give it room to naturalize. Narrow lance-shaped leaves line tall, branching stems. Clouds of small white daisies appear from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Stratify seed; thrives in wet soils and rain gardens.

directions: A natural choice for wet meadows, pond edges, and rain gardens, it loves consistently moist to boggy soil. Reddish stems rise tall and may need support in rich sites. Pale blue-violet daisies bloom generously in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Stratify seed, or divide in spring; parent of many dwarf hybrids.

directions: This compact, bushy native tolerates a range of soils and forms neat mounds. Small leaves clothe wiry, much-branched stems. Pale blue to white daisies cover the plant in autumn, and it is a parent of many dwarf border asters.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: This selection flowers heavily with denser sprays than the wild species. Give it woodland-edge conditions with morning sun and moist, humusy soil. A profusion of small pale-blue daisies covers the plant in early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: This selection trades the usual white for soft pink flowers on airy, arching stems. Give it full sun and dry, well-drained soil for the best display. Tiny pink daisies fill the plant in a cloudlike haze through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist soil.

directions: This old favorite carries unusual double, powder-blue flowers on sturdy stems. Keep the soil evenly moist and divide every few years to fight mildew. Fully double lavender-blue daisies open in a long autumn display.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Valued for its rich, glowing color among the New York asters, it needs fertile soil and steady moisture. Improve airflow and divide regularly to limit powdery mildew. Ruby-red to crimson daisies blanket the plant in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist soil; pinch to control height.

directions: This tall, long-grown selection offers clear pink rather than the usual purple. Pinch in early summer and keep soil moist to prevent lower-leaf loss. Soft salmon-pink daisies open in profusion in September and October.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist soil.

directions: A tall selection whose deep color stands out in the fall border, it wants fertile, evenly moist soil. Pinch once in June and stake if the site is exposed. Rich ruby-red daisies with gold eyes bloom heavily in early autumn.

planting.note: Stratify seed; likes moist woodland soil and spreads by rhizomes.

directions: Named for its distinctively zigzag stems, this native colonizes moist woodland edges and stream banks. Give it part shade and steady moisture, allowing room to spread. Pale blue-violet daisies open in loose clusters in early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist soil.

directions: This selection offers large, soft pink daisies on strong stems. Keep the soil moist and pinch in early summer to reduce flopping. Clear pink flowers with yellow centers open freely from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist soil; a compact dwarf.

directions: This dwarf selection makes a tidy front-of-border mound smothered in bloom. Keep it evenly moist and divide often to keep mildew at bay. Semi-double reddish-purple daisies cover the plant in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: A low, mounding selection in an unusual pink, it wants sharp drainage and full sun. Avoid rich, wet soil that causes floppy growth. Soft pink daisies cover the compact mounds in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Stratify seed; adaptable native for meadows and borders.

directions: This mildew-resistant native carries smooth, glaucous foliage and stiff upright stems. Give it full sun and average to dry soil; it needs no staking. Star-shaped blue-violet daisies open across the plant in autumn.

planting.note: Stratify seed; tough pioneer of dry, open ground.

directions: An adaptable native pioneer, it thrives in dry, disturbed, sunny sites and self-sows freely. Hairy stems branch into wide sprays late in the season. Countless tiny white daisies with yellow centers appear from late summer through frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in humus-rich, consistently moist soil.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of a moist, peaty mix and leave it uncovered under bright indirect light. Keep the crown mulched and never let the roots dry out during summer heat. Upright white to rosy plumes bloom in early summer, a bit ahead of many hybrids.

planting.note: Site in dappled shade with rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto damp soil and keep it evenly moist and lightly shaded until seedlings appear. Feed in spring and mulch to hold moisture through the growing season. Loose, gracefully arching plumes open in summer and are the parents of many drooping garden forms.

planting.note: Plant this compact species at the front of a shady, moist bed.

directions: Sow seed thinly on the soil surface and mist to settle it without burying. This small, glossy-leaved species stays neat and pairs well with hostas along a border edge. Airy, star-shaped pink flowers appear in late summer.

planting.note: Use as a tall late-season accent in moist ground.

directions: Surface-sow and keep bright and moist; seedlings develop slowly their first year. This tall variety extends the astilbe season and handles more sun than typical types. Narrow upright spikes of purple bloom in late summer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant as a spreading groundcover in part shade.

directions: Press seed onto moist soil and grow on in bright shade until plants knit together. This low, rhizomatous form slowly colonizes to form a weed-suppressing mat. Short mauve-pink plumes stand above the mat in late summer.

planting.note: Native woodlander for rich, moist shade gardens.

directions: Cold-stratify seed for a few weeks, then surface-sow and keep shaded and moist. This is the only astilbe native to North America and forms a large, shrubby clump in woodland conditions. Airy cream-white panicles open in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Tiny species suited to rock gardens and troughs.

directions: Surface-sow the dust-fine seed and keep constantly damp in a shaded frame. This miniature species needs sharp yet moisture-retentive soil and shelter from hot sun. Delicate pink plumes appear low over bronze-tinged foliage in summer.

planting.note: Bold species for the back of a moist border.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep moist and shaded until strong seedlings form. Give this large species room and steady moisture to reach full size. Broad creamy-white panicles rise well above the coarse foliage in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Streamside giant for consistently wet ground.

directions: Surface-sow seed and keep the flat wet, mimicking the plant's native riverbanks. This towering species thrives in boggy, humus-rich soil and part shade. Nodding greenish-white plumes open in summer, followed by ornamental reddish seed heads.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set divisions with the crown just at soil level and water in well. This hybrid group is the classic garden astilbe, needing dependable summer moisture and part shade. Full, upright plumes in white, pink, or red bloom across early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shaded ground.

directions: Plant the division with its crown at the surface and keep the soil evenly moist through summer. Bronze-green new foliage sets off the flowers, and clumps benefit from division every few years. Deep blood-red plumes appear in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set out in spring in rich, damp soil in part shade.

directions: Plant divisions level with the ground and mulch to conserve moisture. An early bloomer, it rewards consistent watering with dense flowering. Bright white, upright plumes open in early summer above glossy dark foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and keep roots cool and moist all season. This variety, known in Germany as Brautschleier, forms graceful arching heads. Feathery pure-white plumes bloom in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist part shade.

directions: Plant with the crown just at the surface and water deeply during dry spells. This taller hybrid makes an airy, upright clump good for the middle of a border. Loose lilac-purple plumes open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in consistently moist, rich soil.

directions: Set divisions at ground level and keep evenly watered through summer. An early-flowering type that holds its color well in light shade. Clear rose-pink plumes bloom in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in damp, fertile soil in part shade.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and mulch to keep the roots cool. This old favorite is compact and free-flowering with reliable summer moisture. Soft salmon-pink plumes appear in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist ground.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and provide steady moisture in summer. A vigorous, tall hybrid valued as a cut flower. Arching rose-pink plumes bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moisture-retentive soil in part shade.

directions: Plant with the crown at the surface and water through dry weather. This tall variety adds strong vertical form to a shaded bed. Orchid-pink plumes open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, consistently damp soil.

directions: Set divisions at ground level and keep well watered. Reddish-tinged foliage complements the flowers, and clumps flower reliably in part shade. Bright red, upright plumes open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates more sun and drier soil than most.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and water while establishing. This compact chinensis selection has bronze-green foliage and takes heat better than hybrids. Dense, fragrant raspberry-purple plumes bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade; tolerant of drier soil.

directions: Plant level with the soil surface and water in well. Blue-green foliage with reddish stems sets off the flowers, and it handles heat well. Soft pink plumes open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; more sun- and drought-tolerant than hybrids.

directions: Set the crown at ground level and keep watered until roots take hold. Dark bronze-red foliage adds contrast, and plants tolerate heat and some drought. Deep red-purple plumes bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade with even moisture.

directions: Plant the division at soil level and water regularly to establish. Dark stems and bronze new growth frame the flowers on this vigorous selection. Full white plumes open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tall late-season accent.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and water during establishment. This tall, late variety extends bloom into the end of the season and tolerates some sun. Narrow, erect purple-red spikes open in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; excellent tall type for moist ground.

directions: Set the crown at the surface and keep moist while it establishes. This robust, upright variety makes a strong vertical statement at the back of a bed. Dense magenta-purple plumes bloom in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a shady, moist border.

directions: Plant the small division level with the soil and keep evenly moist. Named Perennial Plant of the Year in 1994, it forms a tidy mound of dark, ferny foliage. Airy shell-pink plumes arch over the leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded ground.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and mulch to hold moisture. This compact simplicifolia type has glossy dark foliage and a graceful habit. Loose, arching pale pink plumes bloom in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist part shade.

directions: Set the crown at the surface and keep the soil reliably moist. Prized for its distinctive drooping flower heads that arch like plumes. Coral-pink cascading plumes open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, moist soil in part shade.

directions: Plant the crown level with the ground and water well through summer. This tall variety makes a bold clump with large, nodding flower heads. Arching creamy-white plumes bloom in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown as much for foliage as for bloom.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and keep moist for best leaf color. Foliage emerges green, shifts to burgundy and purple through the season, and colors best with some sun. Light pink plumes appear in midsummer above the changing leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; prized for dark, glossy foliage.

directions: Plant the crown at the surface and keep the soil evenly moist. The chocolate-purple, lacquered foliage holds its color all season and is the main draw. Soft pink plumes rise above the dark leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil in part shade.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and mulch to keep roots cool. Blue-green foliage with red stems and veining makes this striking even out of bloom. Apricot-pink plumes on deep red stems open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in consistently moist soil.

directions: Plant the crown level with the ground and water through dry weather. Dark bronze-green foliage complements the flowers on this reliable variety. Deep red plumes bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates slightly drier soil once set.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and water while establishing. A vigorous, adaptable variety that performs well in less-than-ideal moisture. Rose-red plumes open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil in part shade.

directions: Set the crown at the surface and keep evenly watered. Known in Germany as Glut, this late variety holds intense color in the shade garden. Deep, glowing red plumes bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in damp, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and mulch to conserve moisture. One of the purest white astilbes, with fresh green foliage as a backdrop. Dense white plumes bloom in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in part shade.

directions: Plant the crown level with the ground and water deeply during dry spells. This tall white variety, known as Diamant, gives a bright vertical accent. Sparkling white plumes open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; more heat- and sun-tolerant than hybrids.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and water to establish. This chinensis selection takes heat and some sun better than most astilbes. Bright reddish-purple plumes bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil in part shade.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and keep evenly moist. Part of the compact, floriferous Younique series that flowers young and freely. Broad silvery-pink plumes open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in consistently moist soil.

directions: Plant the crown level with the ground and water through summer heat. A compact white variety that flowers freely in the shade garden. Bright white plumes bloom in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tall, late chinensis type.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and water to establish. This tall selection tolerates some sun and drier soil and blooms late in the season. Plumes open pale creamy-yellow and mature to soft pink in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a part-shade bed.

directions: Plant the small crown at soil level and water until settled. A dwarf chinensis form with bronze-tinged foliage that tolerates heat well. Compact rose-pink plumes bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil in part shade.

directions: Plant the crown at the surface and keep the soil moist through summer. Dark green foliage sets off the intensely colored flowers on this classic variety. Deep crimson-red plumes bloom in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow after a cold-moist chill, or divide crowns in early spring or autumn.

directions: Give seed six to eight weeks of cold, damp stratification before sowing barely below the surface, as fresh seed germinates unevenly. Set plants in humus-rich, reliably moist soil in dappled shade and mulch to keep roots cool. Pincushion flowers ringed by papery bracts open from early to midsummer and rebloom if deadheaded.

planting.note: Chill seed before sowing or lift and divide established clumps in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify seed for several weeks, then sow shallowly and keep evenly moist during the slow, patchy germination. This species spreads by creeping rhizomes, so plant it in consistently damp ground where it can knit into a colony. Look for its notably broad, shell-pink bracts and clear rose flowers in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow chilled seed in spring or divide the compact crowns as growth resumes.

directions: Provide a cold-moist chilling period, then surface-sow and be patient with erratic sprouting. This is a daintier, smaller masterwort suited to the front of a shaded, moist border. Delicate white flowers backed by fine green-tipped bracts appear from early summer.

planting.note: Best from chilled seed sown in spring; also divides cleanly in early spring.

directions: Chill seed several weeks before sowing thinly, keeping the mix cool and just moist. This alpine species is the smallest masterwort, at home in a cool woodland edge or rock garden with gritty, humus-rich soil. Small white-and-pink flower heads open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Propagate by dividing crowns in early spring; does not come true from seed.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown level at the soil surface in cool, moisture-retentive ground enriched with leaf mould. Named for its exceptionally long, green-tipped white bracts that give a spidery, elongated look. Cut back after the first flush in early summer to encourage a second round of bloom.

planting.note: Increase only by division in spring; this sterile-leaning selection is grown vegetatively.

directions: Set divisions in fertile, moisture-holding soil in part shade and water through dry spells. A long-flowering pink selection prized for staying in bloom over many weeks. Deadhead spent heads to keep the soft rose flowers coming from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Divide crowns in early spring to keep the deep colour true.

directions: Plant divisions in rich, damp soil where afternoon shade protects the dark flowers from fading. One of the deepest red masterworts, with wine-coloured heads and bracts held on dark stems. Trim after flowering to tidy the clump and prompt fresh blooms.

planting.note: Propagate by spring division to preserve the rich red tone.

directions: Give it cool, retentive soil and light shade to hold its saturated colour longest. Dark burgundy flowers and matching bracts sit above green foliage on wiry stems. Shear spent stems to refresh the plant and extend bloom through summer.

planting.note: Grown from spring divisions; seedlings will not repeat the parent.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, evenly moist ground with some shade to intensify the colour. This classic selection carries dark blood-red flowers on deep-toned stems. Cut back after the main flush to encourage a lighter second showing later in the season.

planting.note: Divide in early spring to maintain the cultivar.

directions: Set plants in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in dappled light. Soft ruby-pink flowers are held airily above the mound of divided leaves. Deadhead regularly and it will flower generously from early summer onward.

planting.note: Increase by spring division of the crown.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, damp, organic soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Part of the Star series, it bears large deep crimson-red flower heads with pointed bracts. Remove faded heads to keep the compact clump blooming through summer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions taken in early spring.

directions: Give it moist, fertile soil in part shade and steady watering. This Star-series selection produces clean white flowers with green-tipped, star-shaped bracts. Deadheading keeps the show going and prevents excess self-seeding.

planting.note: Divide crowns in spring to keep the vivid colour.

directions: Plant in retentive, humus-rich soil with light shade to hold the intense hue. A bold Star-series masterwort with bright red flowers over dark-flushed stems. Shear back after the first flush to freshen the foliage and spur more bloom.

planting.note: Divide in spring; variegation is only kept by vegetative propagation.

directions: Site divisions where morning sun brings out the cream-and-yellow leaf margins without scorching them. The best variegation shows in spring before the pale pink flowers open in summer. Remove any all-green shoots promptly so the plant does not revert.

planting.note: Propagate by early-spring division.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, fertile soil in dappled shade for best flower quality. Large pure-white heads with broad, green-tipped bracts light up a shaded border. Deadhead to prolong flowering and to limit self-sown seedlings.

planting.note: Divide crowns in early spring to maintain the selection.

directions: Set divisions in damp, humus-rich soil and mulch the crowns each spring. Selected for its generously sized white flower heads with a faint green cast. Cut back spent stems to keep the plant tidy and encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Best grown from spring divisions for consistent white flowers.

directions: Plant in moisture-retentive, fertile soil in part shade and keep the roots cool with mulch. A clean white-flowered form that glows in shaded plantings and cuts well for the vase. Deadhead regularly to lengthen the flowering season into late summer.

planting.note: Divide in spring; seedlings vary and will not reliably repeat the red.

directions: Grow in cool, damp, organic soil with light shade to deepen the colour. An old red-flowered selection with dusky rose to wine heads over green foliage. Shear after the first flush to tidy the clump and prompt more flowers.

planting.note: Propagate by spring division for uniform pink colour.

directions: Plant in fertile, retentive soil in dappled light and water in dry weather. Soft pink flowers with pale bracts suit cottage and woodland-edge borders. Remove faded heads to keep new blooms forming through summer.

planting.note: Increase by division of the crown in early spring.

directions: Give it moist, humus-rich soil in part shade and steady moisture. A vigorous, free-flowering selection with deep pink to rose-red heads. Deadhead through the season to keep the long display going.

planting.note: Divide in spring to preserve the rich red colour.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, retentive soil with some shade to hold the deep tone. Dark ruby-red flowers on sturdy stems make it a strong border and cutting plant. Shear spent heads to encourage a second flush later in summer.

planting.note: Grown from spring divisions.

directions: Set in fertile, moisture-holding soil in dappled shade. Deep pink-red flowers are carried well above the leafy mound. Deadhead regularly to prolong the summer display.

planting.note: Propagate by spring division to keep the dark colour.

directions: Plant in cool, damp, organic soil and give light shade to deepen the flowers. One of the darkest selections, with intense blood-red heads and dusky stems. Cut back after flowering to refresh the clump.

planting.note: Increase by early-spring division.

directions: Give it fertile, retentive soil in part shade and consistent moisture. A long-flowering pink selection with a soft silvery sheen to the bracts. Deadhead often and it will bloom freely for many weeks in summer.

planting.note: Divide in spring; the dark bracts do not come true from seed.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil where light shade keeps the near-black bracts from fading. The flowers are deep wine-red with strikingly dark, glossy bracts beneath. Shear after the first flush to encourage more of the dramatic blooms.

planting.note: Best kept by spring division.

directions: Set divisions in cool, damp, fertile soil in dappled light. Rich red-flowered form with deep-coloured bracts on strong stems. Remove spent heads to keep the plant flowering and to reduce seedlings.

planting.note: Propagate by spring division.

directions: Plant in retentive, humus-rich soil in part shade and water in dry weather. Clear rose-pink flowers are held generously above fresh green foliage. Deadhead through the season to keep the blooms coming.

planting.note: Grown from divisions taken in spring.

directions: Give it cool, damp, fertile ground in dappled shade. Soft pink flowers with pale bracts make an easy, long-flowering border plant. Deadhead regularly to lengthen the display and tidy the clump.

planting.note: Divide the compact crowns in early spring.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, moist, gritty-humus soil at a shaded border edge. A dainty, smaller masterwort with deep red flowers over fine foliage. Deadhead to keep the neat clump blooming through summer.

planting.note: Propagate by division in early spring.

directions: Set divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in part shade. A Star-series selection with bright red flowers and pointed, star-like bracts. Cut back spent stems to freshen the plant and prompt repeat bloom.

planting.note: Divide in spring to keep the dark colour true.

directions: Plant in cool, damp, humus-rich soil with light shade to deepen the flowers. Very dark red, almost black-red heads on dusky stems give a moody effect. Shear after the main flush to encourage further bloom.

planting.note: Divide in spring; the variegation is kept only by vegetative propagation.

directions: Site divisions where gentle morning light brings out the cream-edged foliage without scorching. Grown mainly for its bold variegated leaves, topped by pale pink flowers in summer. Remove any plain green shoots to stop the plant reverting.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set plants out after the last hard frost.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of gritty, well-drained mix and leave it uncovered, since light aids germination. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and shear the mat back hard right after flowering to keep it dense. Expect a carpet of small four-petaled blooms from early to mid spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter; this hybrid group includes most modern garden strains.

directions: Surface-sow seed and keep it lightly moist and bright until sprouts appear. Plant into lean, alkaline, gritty soil where it can spill over a wall or edge, and trim after bloom to prevent a woody center. The trailing mounds cover themselves in spring flowers ranging from blue to red across the many named forms.

planting.note: A southern European alpine species best started indoors and moved out in spring.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of a fast-draining alpine mix and do not bury it. Give it a hot, sunny, gravelly spot that stays dry through summer, as it resents wet feet. Violet-blue flowers open in mid spring above grey-green mounds.

planting.note: A compact, small-leaved species suited to troughs and rock crevices.

directions: Sow on the surface and press in gently, keeping the pot in bright, cool conditions. Plant into a crevice or trough with gritty soil and full exposure, where its neat cushions stay tidy. Small violet blooms appear in spring on this dwarf, tight-growing species.

planting.note: A grey-leaved Turkish species that thrives in hot, dry rock gardens.

directions: Surface-sow and hold in a bright, airy spot until seedlings establish. Site it in the leanest, driest, sunniest corner you have, since its felted grey foliage hates humidity. Pale lilac flowers open over the silvery mat in spring.

planting.note: An alpine species for connoisseur rock gardens and scree beds.

directions: Sprinkle seed on the surface of a sharp scree mix and leave uncovered. Plant in full sun with perfect drainage and protect the crown from winter wet with a gravel mulch. Compact cushions carry lilac-purple flowers in early to mid spring.

planting.note: A classic deep violet clone grown from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Set nursery plants or rooted cuttings into gritty, well-drained soil at their original depth. Give full sun and shear the whole plant back by half once the main flush fades to encourage a second showing. This old favorite blankets itself in rich violet-purple flowers each spring.

planting.note: A reliable purple-flowered clone propagated from cuttings or division.

directions: Plant into lean, alkaline soil with sharp drainage, spacing plants so mats can knit together. Trim hard after flowering to keep the cushion tight and avoid bare centers. Expect a dense sheet of rich purple bloom over evergreen foliage in mid spring.

planting.note: A seed-grown strain in the Royal series; sow indoors in late winter.

directions: Press seed onto the mix surface without covering, as it needs light to sprout. Grow on in full sun with gritty drainage and shear spent flowers to tidy the mound. Deep blue-violet flowers smother the compact mats in spring.

planting.note: A carmine-red seed strain in the Royal series.

directions: Surface-sow and keep bright and evenly moist until germination. Move seedlings to a sunny, sharply drained bed or wall edge after frost danger passes. Reddish-carmine flowers cover the trailing cushions in mid spring.

planting.note: A violet-flowered seed strain in the Royal series.

directions: Leave seed uncovered on the surface and provide bright light for germination. Plant out into full sun with lean, gritty soil, then cut back after the spring flush. Clear violet blooms carpet the low mound each spring.

planting.note: A trailing seed strain in the Cascade series, ideal for walls.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep it lit and lightly moist until it sprouts. Site plants at the top of a wall or slope so the stems can cascade downward, and shear after bloom. Purple flowers drape over the trailing foliage in spring.

planting.note: A red-flowered trailing seed strain in the Cascade series.

directions: Press seed onto the mix and leave uncovered under bright light. Plant where it can spill over a rock ledge or raised edge in full sun with sharp drainage. Rosy-red flowers tumble across the mat in mid spring.

planting.note: A blue-toned trailing seed strain in the Cascade series.

directions: Surface-sow and hold under bright conditions until germination is complete. Grow in full sun with gritty, alkaline soil at a wall top so growth trails freely. Blue-violet blooms cover the cascading stems in spring.

planting.note: A uniform blue seed strain that can flower in its first season if started early.

directions: Sow onto the surface and keep bright, since darkness suppresses germination. Grow on cool and set out in full sun with excellent drainage once frost has passed. Large blue flowers cover the tidy mounds in spring.

planting.note: A vigorous seed strain in the Audrey series with early bloom.

directions: Press seed lightly onto the surface and keep it exposed to light while germinating. Transplant to full sun in lean, sharply drained soil and shear back after the first flush. Bright blue flowers smother the low cushions in spring.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings to keep its cream-edged variegated leaves.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun to hold the variegation strong. Remove any all-green shoots that appear so they do not overtake the cream-edged foliage. Lilac-purple flowers rise above the bright leaves in spring, which stay attractive year-round.

planting.note: A double pink clone increased by cuttings, not seed.

directions: Set plants into sharply drained, alkaline soil in full sun and avoid summer waterlogging. Shear the mat back after flowering to keep it compact and free of woody gaps. Fully double pink flowers cover the cushion in mid spring.

planting.note: A soft rose-pink clone propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant into lean, gritty soil in full sun where drainage is fast. Trim spent flowers and stems after bloom to encourage a tight, renewed cushion. Warm rose-pink flowers cover the mound in spring.

planting.note: A rich purple clone grown from cuttings or division.

directions: Establish plants in a sunny, sharply drained spot such as a wall or raised bed. Cut the whole plant back by half once the main display finishes to keep it dense. Deep purple flowers cover the vigorous mat in mid spring.

planting.note: A white-eyed purple clone kept true by cuttings.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Shear after flowering to renew the cushion and prevent an open, woody center. Purple flowers with a striking pale-white eye cover the mound in spring.

planting.note: A compact deep-violet clone propagated from cuttings.

directions: Set plants into sharply drained, sunny ground and keep them lean for the tightest growth. Trim after bloom to maintain the neat, low profile this form is valued for. Deep violet-blue flowers blanket the compact cushion in spring.

planting.note: An old carmine-pink clone increased from cuttings.

directions: Plant into gritty, alkaline soil in full sun with excellent drainage. Cut back hard after the first flush to keep the cushion tidy and encourage rebloom. Bright carmine-pink flowers cover the mat in mid spring.

planting.note: An old rosy-red clone propagated vegetatively.

directions: Establish plants in full sun in lean, fast-draining soil, spacing them to knit into a carpet. Shear spent flowers and stems after bloom to prevent a bare middle. Rosy-red flowers cover the trailing mound in spring.

planting.note: A large-flowered purple clone kept true by cuttings.

directions: Plant into sharply drained, sunny soil and avoid rich, moisture-holding beds. Trim the plant back after the main display to renew tight, healthy growth. Large purple-violet flowers cover the cushion in spring.

planting.note: A well-branched seed strain in the Rock On series.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it bright, as light is needed to trigger germination. Grow cool, then plant out in full sun with gritty drainage after frost. Blue flowers cover the well-branched, compact mounds in spring.

planting.note: A purple seed strain in the Rock On series.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and leave uncovered under bright light. Set plants in full sun in lean, sharply drained soil once the weather warms. Purple flowers smother the neat, compact cushions in spring.

planting.note: A dwarf, early seed strain in the compact Kitte series.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep bright and lightly moist until seedlings emerge. Grow in full sun with excellent drainage, ideal for troughs, edging, and small rockeries. Blue flowers cover the tight, low cushions in early spring.

planting.note: An old reddish-purple clone maintained from cuttings.

directions: Plant into gritty, sunny, well-drained ground and keep the soil lean. Shear the mat after flowering to hold a compact, weed-free carpet. Reddish-purple flowers cover the spreading mound in spring.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in autumn so winter cold breaks dormancy, or nick and soak seed before a spring sowing.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in a permanent, sunny spot since the deep taproot resents transplanting once established. Water through the first summer while roots develop, then let the plant fend for itself. Expect the first indigo-blue spires in late spring of the second or third year, followed by charcoal seed pods that rattle in fall.

planting.note: Fall-sow outdoors or cold-stratify seed for a few weeks before spring planting.

directions: Plant this compact prairie form a quarter inch deep in lean, well-drained soil and full sun. It stays shorter and stockier than the species, rarely needing staking. Blue flower spikes open in late spring above blue-green foliage, giving way to inflated pods.

planting.note: Best fall-sown so seed experiences natural winter chilling.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in full sun and average soil, allowing plenty of room for the broad, shrubby clump. Tall white flower spires rise in late spring and early summer. Once rooted it is long-lived and drought tolerant, so avoid disturbing the taproot.

planting.note: Fall-sow or stratify seed before spring planting.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in full sun and sharply drained soil. Bright buttery-yellow spikes appear in late spring above blue-green foliage, followed by nearly round pods. It forms a tidy dome and shrugs off summer heat and drought.

planting.note: Sow in autumn to satisfy the seed's cold requirement.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in full sun and lean soil. This low, wide-spreading species holds arching sprays of creamy-yellow flowers close to the ground in early spring, often the first Baptisia to bloom. Give it space to sprawl and leave it undisturbed.

planting.note: Fall-sow into sandy soil where the deep root can run down.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun. This unusual species has round blue-green leaves pierced by the stem and small yellow flowers tucked in the leaf axils. The arching, zigzag stems are prized for cut arrangements.

planting.note: Sow in fall in sandy soil for natural stratification.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in sandy, well-drained soil and full sun. Large clear-yellow flowers open on upright racemes in mid to late spring. This southeastern species is compact and heat tolerant, well suited to sandy coastal gardens.

planting.note: Fall-sow so the seed coat weathers over winter.

directions: Set seed a quarter inch deep in lean, well-drained soil and full sun. Slender spikes of small white flowers rise well above the foliage in late spring. It is a graceful, understated species for meadow and native plantings across the Southeast.

planting.note: Fall-sow fresh seed as soon as pods mature.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in rich, moist but well-drained woodland soil with morning sun and afternoon shade. Cream to pale-yellow flowers open in spring and swell into notably large, papery pods. This rare river-bottom species appreciates a little more moisture than most Baptisia.

planting.note: Sow in autumn into sandy, sharply drained ground.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep in sandy, dry soil and full sun. Small bright-yellow flowers are scattered singly along the branching stems in spring. This tough southern-plains species thrives on neglect once established.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring in a permanent, sunny site.

directions: Set container-grown plants at the depth they grew, spacing generously for the large vase-shaped clump. This celebrated hybrid carries smoky violet-gray flowers on charcoal stems in late spring. Give it full sun and leave the taproot undisturbed for a decades-long performer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where they can stay put.

directions: Plant at the original soil level in full sun and well-drained ground. Tall spires of soft butter-yellow flowers rise above blue-green foliage in late spring. It forms a shrubby mound that may benefit from a light support ring in rich soil.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring in a large permanent spot.

directions: Set plants at the depth they grew and allow room for a big, robust clump. Bicolor flowers of dusky violet-purple with yellow keels appear in late spring. This vigorous hybrid makes a bold background specimen in full sun.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the crown's original depth in well-drained soil and full sun. Flowers open lemon-yellow and age to burnt orange and red, so the spikes show several tones at once. Leave undisturbed and enjoy the color shift through late spring.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Soft periwinkle-blue flowers with cream keels open on upright spikes in late spring. This early-flowering hybrid forms a neat, rounded clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny permanent site.

directions: Plant at the crown's original depth in full sun and well-drained soil. Deep violet-blue flowers crowd the tall spikes in late spring. It is long-lived and drought tolerant once the taproot establishes.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring.

directions: Set plants at the depth they grew in full sun and sharply drained soil. Vivid, saturated yellow flowers cover the plant in late spring and are followed by rounded pods. This selection forms a dense, tidy dome and tolerates heat and drought.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the original crown depth in full sun and well-drained soil. Bicolor flowers of maroon and yellow open in late spring on a bushy, well-branched clump. Leave the taproot undisturbed for a reliable long-lived performer.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Rich chocolate-purple flowers rise on dark stems in late spring. This compact selection keeps a dense, mounded habit that rarely needs staking.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the crown's original level in full sun and well-drained soil. Bright lemon-yellow flowers stand on contrasting charcoal-gray stems in late spring. It forms a dense, rounded clump and looks handsome long after bloom.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in full sun and well-drained soil. Rich indigo-blue flowers pack tall spikes in late spring above a compact, upright clump. Its narrow footprint suits smaller borders where the species would be too large.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny spot.

directions: Plant at the original crown depth in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Deep sapphire-blue flowers cover the plant in late spring on a bushy, well-branched frame. Leave it in place to bulk up into a long-lived clump.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Set plants at the depth they grew in full sun and well-drained soil. Buds open pale butter-yellow and fade to soft creamy white, giving a two-tone effect on each spike in late spring. It keeps a low, rounded habit ideal for the front of a border.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the original crown level in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing room for a large clump. Flowers open soft yellow and age through smoky rose to deep raspberry, so the tall spikes carry several colors at once. Leave the taproot undisturbed for years of bloom.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Soft pink flowers with darker keels rise on tall spikes above a robust, upright clump in late spring. It makes a substantial specimen once mature, so give it space.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the original crown depth in full sun and well-drained soil. Large bright-yellow flowers stand on tall spikes above a big, vigorous clump in late spring. This robust selection makes a bold background plant that shrugs off heat.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring in a sunny spot.

directions: Set plants at the depth they grew in full sun and well-drained soil. Long spikes of clear yellow flowers tower above the foliage in late spring. It forms a large, upright clump that anchors the back of a border.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the original crown level in full sun and well-drained soil. Vivid violet-purple flowers appear on a low, dense mound in late spring. Its compact, spreading habit makes it one of the shortest cultivars, well suited to smaller gardens.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in full sun and well-drained soil. Flowers blend warm yellow, amber, and rusty-brown tones on the same spike in late spring. It forms a tidy, rounded clump with a rich autumnal color palette.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the original crown depth in full sun and well-drained soil. Rounded spikes of purple-blue flowers cover a compact, bushy plant in late spring. Its shorter stature and dense habit make it a good choice where space is limited.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring in a large, permanent, sunny spot.

directions: Set plants at the depth they grew in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing plenty of room for the tall clump. Very long spikes of white flowers on dark stems rise in late spring, taller than typical white wild indigo. Give it a support ring in rich soil or let it stand on its own in leaner ground.

planting.note: Set out divisions or seedlings in spring once the soil has warmed and drained.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and press it in without covering, since light aids sprouting, and keep the tray cool and evenly damp. Plant clumps with the fleshy rhizome resting just at the soil surface in humus-rich ground. Rosy pink bell-shaped flowers rise on stout stalks in early spring above the glossy, paddle-shaped leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in ordinary garden soil that stays slightly moist.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of a cool seed mix and leave it uncovered under bright indirect light. Space plants so the thick evergreen rosettes can knit into a weed-smothering groundcover, and cut spent flower spikes to keep clumps tidy. Nodding pink flowers appear from late winter into spring, often reblooming lightly in mild autumns.

planting.note: Site in a sheltered, part-shaded spot as the large soft leaves resent hard frost and drying wind.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and hold it at cool room temperature with steady moisture until sprouts appear. Give this species a moist, humus-rich pocket sheltered from cold winds, since its broad hairy leaves are the most frost-tender in the genus. Blush-white to soft pink flowers open in early spring, sometimes before the new foliage fully expands.

planting.note: Suited to a rock garden or front-of-border pocket with sharp drainage.

directions: Press the seed onto a gritty, well-drained mix and leave it uncovered in bright light. This is a compact, tidy species that fits smaller spaces and gritty raised beds. Fragrant pink to near-white flowers cluster on short stems in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Give a warm, sheltered, part-shaded site as this species is less cold-tolerant.

directions: Surface-sow and keep cool and lightly moist until germination, then grow on in bright shade. This rarer Chinese species carries clean white flowers and needs protection from hard freezes at the colder edge of its range. Slender spikes of white blooms open in spring above glossy green rosettes.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall with the rhizome at the surface.

directions: Set the fleshy rhizome so its top sits at soil level and firm it into humus-rich ground. The rounded leaves turn burgundy-purple through cold weather, giving strong winter interest. Magenta-pink bells rise on ruddy stalks in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in part shade for the cleanest flower color.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown resting at the surface and mulch lightly to hold moisture. Pure white flowers that age to pale pink open above bold evergreen leaves. Shear old flower stems after bloom to keep the clump neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring where cool nights deepen the winter leaf color.

directions: Settle divisions at soil level in rich, moisture-retentive ground. The rounded leaves flush deep ruby-maroon in autumn and winter, backed in spring by rich rosy-red bells. Remove tattered leaves in early spring to showcase the new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to protect the softer flower tint from bleaching.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly and keep the soil evenly moist through the first season. Warm salmon-pink flowers, unusual for the genus, open in mid-spring over glossy foliage. Divide crowded clumps every few years to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile soil; tolerates full sun where summers are cool.

directions: Set divisions with the crown at the surface and water in well. This compact selection, also sold as Evening Glow, carries semi-double reddish-purple flowers and leaves that bronze richly in winter. Cut back spent bloom stalks to tidy the low mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade; often reblooms if kept moist.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in rich, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Known also as Morning Blush, it bears deep rose-red flowers and frequently pushes a second flush later in the season. Deadhead the first spikes to encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to keep the white flowers from scorching.

directions: Set divisions at the surface in humus-rich soil and keep evenly moist. This classic selection, sold as Silver Light, opens white flowers that blush pink as they age, with anthers adding a rosy tint. Trim faded stems after flowering finishes.

planting.note: Plant in spring where winter cold intensifies the foliage color.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil and firm gently. Also known as Winter Glow, it turns deep red-bronze in cold weather and follows with rich crimson-red spring flowers. Remove any winter-burned leaves as new growth emerges.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its smaller size suits edging and containers.

directions: Set the shallow rhizome at soil level and water in. This compact selection carries soft baby-pink flowers that deepen slightly with age above neat rounded leaves. Its tidy habit makes it easy to use at the front of a border.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile soil for reliable early bloom.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface and keep steadily moist the first year. Valued for early, deep magenta-rose flowers and glossy leaves that redden in winter. Cut old stems after bloom to keep the clump looking fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade with even moisture.

directions: Settle the rhizome at soil level and mulch lightly. This upright selection bears rich rosy-red flowers on strong stems and leaves that flush maroon in cold weather. Divide every few years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates sun in cool climates and dry shade once established.

directions: Plant divisions at the surface in reasonably fertile soil. Prized for coppery-red winter foliage and lilac-carmine flowers on red stalks in spring. This tough selection copes well with drier shade once it has rooted in.

planting.note: Site in a sheltered spot as the large leaves can be wind-battered.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in fertile ground and shelter from strong wind. This large, bold selection bears big glossy leaves and tall stems of vivid crimson-red flowers. Give it room to spread and protect the foliage from harsh exposure.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set divisions with the rhizome at the surface and keep moist while establishing. This selection offers deep rose-red flowers and leaves that take on bronze-red tones in winter. Remove spent flower stems to tidy the plant after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring where cold weather brings out the two-toned foliage.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and mulch lightly for winter. Its twisted leaves show green above and red-purple beneath, giving a distinctive winter look, followed by rosy-red spring flowers. Clear away weathered foliage as fresh growth begins.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile soil in part shade.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface and firm gently. This robust selection carries large purple-red leaves in winter and tall spikes of rich magenta-pink flowers. Cut back old bloom stalks and tattered leaves in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring for spring bloom plus strong fall and winter leaf color.

directions: Set the rhizome at soil level in humus-rich ground and keep moist. Valued for rosy-pink spring flowers and leaves that turn burgundy and bronze from autumn onward. Trim spent stems to keep the foliage display clean.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the narrow leaves suit smaller borders and containers.

directions: Plant divisions at the surface in well-drained soil and water in. This selection has slender, upright leaves and bright pink flowers on red stems, often reblooming in cool spells. Remove faded stalks to encourage repeat flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and keep evenly moist while establishing. This compact selection bears double, cherry-blossom-pink flowers that give a fuller look than most bergenias. Deadhead spent stems to keep the display tidy and prompt rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to keep the pale flowers from fading fast.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface and water in well. This selection carries upright narrow foliage and soft white-to-blush flowers that age with pink tints. Its long bloom window is extended by removing spent stalks.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in part shade.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and mulch to hold moisture. This selection produces vivid deep-pink flowers held well above the glossy foliage. Cut back spent flower stems and refresh with division as clumps age.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to protect the yellow spring foliage.

directions: Plant divisions at the surface in humus-rich soil and shade from hot afternoon sun. New leaves emerge glowing gold before maturing to green, paired with rosy-pink flowers. Site it in bright, indirect light to keep the young foliage from scorching.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to keep the variegation bright.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in fertile, well-drained ground. This variegated selection has leaves edged in creamy yellow that flush pink in cold weather, with rosy-pink flowers in spring. Remove any all-green leaves to preserve the variegated effect.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the glossy leaves shine best in bright shade.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface and water in. Named for its high-gloss foliage, it turns burgundy in winter and carries rose-pink flowers in spring. Wipe or trim away winter-damaged leaves to show off the polished new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade with steady moisture for best rebloom.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and keep the ground evenly moist. This compact selection is noted for a heavy flush of bright pink flowers and a tendency to rebloom. Deadhead spent stems promptly to keep new buds coming.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to keep the marbled variegation strong.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface in fertile soil. This variegated selection has leaves irregularly splashed and streaked with yellow, taking on pink and red tones in winter, with pink spring flowers. Cut out any plain green leaves so the marbled foliage stays dominant.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set the rhizome at soil level and firm it in. This selection holds tall stems of deep rose-red flowers above glossy green leaves that bronze in winter. Remove spent stalks after bloom to keep the clump neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to protect the pale blooms.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface and keep moist while establishing. This German selection opens white to very pale pink flowers above fresh green foliage. Shear old flower stems once bloom has passed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and water in well. This selection bears white flowers with a rosy-pink calyx, giving a soft two-tone look above deep green leaves. Trim spent flower stems to keep the plant tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile soil in part shade.

directions: Plant divisions at soil level and keep evenly moist. This unusual selection carries double rosy-pink flowers that give a fuller, longer-lasting display than single forms. Remove faded stems to keep the clump looking fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its dwarf habit suits edging and troughs.

directions: Set the small crown at soil level and water in gently. This dwarf selection forms a tight mound of glossy leaves that flush deep red in winter, topped by rosy-pink flowers. Its miniature size makes it ideal for small spaces and container edges.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to bring out the dark leaf edging.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface in humus-rich soil. This selection is grown for leaves rimmed in a dark reddish margin, deepening in winter, with rose-pink spring flowers. Remove weathered outer leaves as new growth appears.

planting.note: Give this large selection room and shelter from strong wind.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in fertile ground and allow generous spacing. This vigorous, large-leaved selection makes a bold groundcover with tall stems of rose-pink flowers in spring. Protect the big leaves from drying wind to keep them looking their best.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring in a shaded, moisture-retentive bed.

directions: Site this silver-frosted selection in part to full shade with rich, evenly moist soil, planting the crown flush with the ground. The heart-shaped leaves are heavily overlaid with silver between green veins, and sprays of blue flowers appear in spring. Named cultivars come true only from division, so lift and split clumps rather than saving seed.

planting.note: Set divisions into cool, shaded, moist ground in early spring.

directions: Give this near-solid silver sport rich, reliably moist soil and protection from hot afternoon sun, which can scorch the pale leaves. Pale blue flowers rise on wiry stems in mid spring above the shimmering foliage. Keep it evenly watered during dry spells and propagate by division to preserve the leaf color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded border with consistently moist, organic soil.

directions: This thick-leaved selection has strong silver overlay and heavier substance that resists scorch and wilting better than older types. Plant the crown level with the soil in part shade with steady moisture. Blue forget-me-not flowers appear in spring, and clumps can be divided in early spring to increase your stock.

planting.note: Set out potted plants in spring into moist, shaded ground.

directions: Prized for thick, vigorous silver leaves with a green vein network, this selection tolerates a touch more sun where soil stays moist. Plant with the crown at soil level and mulch to conserve moisture. Sky-blue flowers open in spring; propagate only by division to keep the foliage true.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded bed with room for its larger footprint.

directions: This oversized selection produces very large silver-frosted leaves and forms a broad clump, so allow extra spacing. Site it in part shade with deep, rich, moisture-retentive soil and keep the crown level with the ground. Blue spring flowers float above the bold foliage; divide mature clumps to propagate.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into cool, shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: This selection shows green leaves accented with silver spotting along the margins between the veins. Grow in part shade with steady moisture, setting the crown at soil level. Blue flowers appear in mid spring, and clumps are easily divided in early spring or after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade, sheltered from wind and hot sun that can scorch variegation.

directions: Green leaves carry an irregular creamy-yellow margin that colors best out of direct sun, which scorches the pale edges. Plant in full or part shade with consistently moist, rich soil and the crown level with the ground. Airy blue flowers open in spring; remove any all-green reverting shoots and propagate by division.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep shade with reliable moisture to protect the white margins.

directions: This old variegated form, also sold as 'Variegata', has broad creamy-white leaf margins that scorch readily in sun or dry soil. Give it a sheltered, shaded spot with rich, evenly moist ground and plant the crown at soil level. Blue spring flowers rise above the foliage; cut out any plain-green reverting leaves and divide to propagate.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded, moist bed away from drying wind.

directions: Grown for green leaves boldly edged in creamy white, this form needs shade and steady moisture or the margins brown at the edges. Set the crown level with the soil in rich, humus-heavy ground. Pale blue flowers appear in spring, and any reverted green shoots should be removed to keep the variegation; divide clumps to increase.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with cool, moist, organic soil.

directions: This selection combines a silver overlay with a creamy leaf margin for a two-tone effect, and it performs best in shade with steady moisture. Plant the crown at soil level in rich, well-drained but moisture-retentive ground. Blue flowers open in spring; protect from hot sun and propagate by division to keep both silver and cream coloring true.
